vix_stress.headline: Volatility channel is contained: VIX 22.22 / OVX 60.28.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Volatility pressure is 49.1. VIX is 22.22, OVX is 60.28, and the oil-versus-equity spread is 38.06 points, so commodity execution friction is present without a full macro panic confirmation.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This sits +12.5 points versus the 36.6 network average, so volatility is a secondary amplifier rather than the lead cost channel.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: Physical availability is not the issue here; the risk is that a calm equity tape can still mask difficult buyer fills if oil volatility re-accelerates first.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Any energy or policy headline can still move the oil complex faster than broad equities because OVX remains materially above VIX.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Managed money stays bullish with net 148,439 contracts, so any volatility re-acceleration can still magnify upside buyer pain.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Escalate this card only if VIX pushes back above 20 or OVX re-expands into the upper-60s while energy stays dominant.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should keep staged execution discipline. The tape is calmer than last week, but it is not calm enough to reward lazy one-window buying.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Detect when commodity volatility is becoming procurement-disruptive before a cost spike fully prints into soybean oil replacement pricing.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A low-VIX but still-elevated OVX regime means soybean-oil buyers can face commodity-specific execution damage before broader macro fear confirms the move.
quantResearchProtocol: Track VIX, OVX, and the OVX minus VIX spread together rather than in isolation.
Flag frictional risk whenever OVX stays above 65 even if VIX is sub-20.
Escalate from friction to disruption only when both volatility complexes start rising together.
Translate the signal into fill-quality and timing-risk language instead of generic volatility commentary.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call this a panic regime while VIX remains below 20.
Do not dismiss commodity stress simply because equities are calm.
Do not present volatility as directional price certainty without linking it to buyer execution risk.
outputRequirements: Quote VIX, OVX, and the spread explicitly.
State whether the regime is calm, frictional, or disruptive.
Give the buyer timing consequence in plain operational language.

notes: Spread-based framing separates broad calm from commodity-specific execution friction.
Narrative stays buyer-side and timing-focused rather than trader-side.

crush_pressure.headline: Board crush at 3.67 and oil-share 55.26% define processor pressure.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Crush pressure is low at 12.1 because board crush is 3.67, oil share is 55.26%, and oil share improved +0.39 points over five sessions.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This channel is -24.5 points versus the network average, making it the calmest lane in the stack.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: Current processor math still supports throughput continuity rather than a margin squeeze or nearby scarcity story.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Trade or biofuel headlines could still reprice this lane, but the present crush math does not independently justify urgency.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Speculative length matters more when processor economics are deteriorating; right now they are acting as a stabilizer instead.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Upgrade only if board crush compresses and oil share rolls over together. That joint move, not either metric alone, would signal buyer trouble.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris can treat crush as background support today rather than a reason to accelerate defensive coverage.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Separate processor-economics watch signals from true near-term availability stress before soybean-oil buyers overreact.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Buyer pain arrives when board crush and oil share deteriorate together; either metric alone can misclassify the procurement state.
quantResearchProtocol: Compute board crush from beans, meal, and oil every refresh.
Track oil-share level and five-session drift alongside the crush margin.
Classify the channel as supportive, compressing, or margin-risk only when both variables align.
Translate the output into buyer pacing instead of generic processor commentary.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call scarcity while board crush remains positive and stable.
Do not treat an oil-share fade by itself as proof of supply stress.
Do not blur profitability risk with physical availability risk.
outputRequirements: Report board crush, oil share, and the short-window oil-share delta.
State whether the channel is margin compression or throughput support.
Tie the conclusion directly to buyer pacing.

sourceRecords.recordHint: ZL closes used in oil-leg valuation
ZM closes used in meal-leg valuation
ZS closes used in soybean input-cost valuation

notes: Board-crush math follows the repo-standard beans, meal, and oil conversion formula.
Oil-share drift remains the faster watch variable than crush level by itself.

china_tension.headline: China-flow sensitivity remains active with CNY at 6.7751.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: China-flow pressure is 43.5. CNY is 6.7751, still 0.2249 below the 7.00 stress anchor, and the seven-day soy-China headline count remains 2.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This sits +6.9 points versus the network average, so it is visible but still below the dominant energy lane.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: China matters when it changes who clears the marginal vegetable-oil barrel equivalent. The FX channel is warmer, but the headline tape still is not confirming a rerouting event.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Tariff rhetoric can still wake this card up quickly, but currency alone is not enough evidence to call a demand-diversion shock.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: With managed money still bullish, a renewed China headline cluster could shorten reaction time even though the current count is still 2.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Keep this on the short watchlist unless CNY weakens toward 7.00 or China-tagged rows start clustering across multiple sessions.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris does not need defensive urgency here today, but this lane can move from quiet to expensive faster than the current headline count suggests.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: China Demand Rerouting and Currency Stress
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Distinguish harmless FX drift from procurement-relevant China-flow stress before the market prices in a true rerouting event.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Currency stress only matters for soybean-oil buyers when it survives multiple sessions and aligns with trade-policy pressure in the soy complex.
quantResearchProtocol: Track CNY level versus the 7.00 anchor and measure five-session change.
Cross-check FX movement against China-tagged narrative density.
Require FX and narrative agreement before escalating the channel.
Translate confirmed stress into buyer reaction-time guidance, not macro theater.
inferenceConstraints: Do not declare a rerouting regime from currency alone.
Do not overstate the channel when China-tagged narrative density is zero.
Do not use generic China-risk wording without specifying the transmission path.
outputRequirements: Quote the currency level and regime classification.
State whether the risk is flow competition, policy escalation, or neither.
Explain the procurement timing consequence in buyer language.

sourceRecords.recordHint: CNY daily close used for flow-sensitivity read

notes: China-flow risk is elevated only when FX and narrative density align.
Current tape does not show China-led confirmation, so the posture remains watchful rather than defensive.

tariff_threat.headline: Policy uncertainty index is 244.89, keeping macro shock risk on watch.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Tariff and macro-policy pressure is 18.3. The uncertainty index printed 244.89, and the seven-day tape carries 3 tariff-tagged rows plus 3 macro-policy rows.
whatsHappening.macroContext: That leaves policy -18.3 points versus the network average: active enough to monitor, but still below energy-driven replacement-cost risk.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: Policy still matters through biofuel economics, trade lanes, and compliance drag, but the visible tape is concentrated rather than broad-based.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: A thin headline set can still matter if it converges with energy stress, which is why this card stays on watch even without a shock reading.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Speculative length gives surprise policy headlines room to travel quickly, but today's 3 tariff-tagged rows do not justify a panic narrative by themselves.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Escalate only if uncertainty stays elevated and re-confirms with energy or volatility transmission over multiple sessions.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should keep policy in the review loop, but today it is a confirmation channel rather than the main reason to rush coverage.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Macro Policy Shock and Geopolitical Escalation
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Determine whether policy uncertainty has crossed the threshold where procurement needs contingency-first execution rather than schedule-first execution.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Policy risk becomes procurement-relevant when uncertainty persists and co-confirms with energy or volatility transmission, not when it appears as a thin standalone headline pulse.
quantResearchProtocol: Track the policy-uncertainty index level and short-window change.
Count tariff- and policy-tagged soy-complex rows in the active window.
Require cross-channel confirmation from energy or volatility before calling a shock regime.
Translate confirmed escalation into optionality-preservation guidance for buyers.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call a shock regime from one data source or one thin headline cluster.
Do not minimize policy risk just because price action is calm.
Do not use vague geopolitical language without a buyer consequence.
outputRequirements: State the uncertainty level and what it implies today.
Provide the buyer posture consequence if escalation occurs.

sourceRecords.recordHint: Economic policy uncertainty index latest print 221.01
seven-day tariff-tagged row count = 1 and macro/policy breadth remains narrow
CL five-session change used as transmission cross-check

notes: Policy risk is framed as a buyer transmission channel, not a generic headline count.
Today the uncertainty series cooled enough that energy and volatility dominate the timing problem.

energy_stress.headline: Crude is 86.12 with 5-day change -7.44% and OVX 60.28.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Energy pass-through leads at 60.0. Crude is 86.12, down -7.44% over five sessions, while OVX still holds 60.28, so rebound risk remains the fastest route back to higher replacement cost.
whatsHappening.macroContext: Energy sits +23.4 points above the 36.6 network average, which keeps it as the dominant buyer-risk channel.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: The issue is not an immediate crude rally today; it is that a market this jumpy can reverse and reprice fryer-oil replacement cost faster than the calmer channels imply.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Any refinery, shipping, or Middle East shock can still reverse the recent crude relief quickly while OVX remains sticky.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: With managed money still bullish, buyers do not get much grace period if energy turns back up from here.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Treat the crude pullback as temporary relief until OVX cools further or crude stops printing multi-session swings of this size.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris can accumulate, but the cadence should stay staged and close to the tape because energy is still the fastest route back to higher replacement cost.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Energy Pass-Through and Buyer Cost Shock
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Measure when crude and energy-volatility pressure are strong enough to alter soybean-oil procurement timing even without a direct soy supply shock.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Buyers often feel crude-led cost anxiety before soybean-oil physical tightness is visible, so energy can dominate procurement pacing even in otherwise moderate risk networks.
quantResearchProtocol: Track crude level, five-session change, and OVX together.
Treat positive crude momentum with elevated OVX as pass-through pressure, not just energy noise.
Compare the energy score against the full network average to measure how dominant it is.
Express the result as timing-risk for a buyer rather than as a trader signal.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call an energy-led crisis without explaining the pass-through path.
Do not ignore crude momentum just because soybean-oil spot is not exploding.
Do not present the card as a physical shortage thesis unless other channels confirm.
outputRequirements: Report crude price, five-session move, and OVX explicitly.
State whether energy is the lead network driver.
Give the buyer timing implication in clear operating language.

sourceRecords.recordHint: WTI crude daily close 96.60 and five-session change -8.37%
managed money remains net long for soybean oil

notes: Energy leads despite crude falling because rebound risk still dominates buyer timing while oil volatility stays elevated.
The card focuses on replacement-cost transmission rather than generic crude commentary.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Collapse the full risk network into one buyer-facing posture without losing the identity of the lead channel.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A soybean-oil buyer needs a single execution posture, but that posture is only valid if it explicitly preserves which channel is dominant and how far it sits above the network average.
quantResearchProtocol: Compute the average cross-driver pressure and the lead-driver spread over that average.
Map the posture threshold to ACCUMULATE, WAIT, or DEFER.
Check whether ZL and crude are moving together or diverging before finalizing the buyer narrative.
Summarize the result in timing-risk language, not speculative-return language.
inferenceConstraints: Do not collapse all drivers into a vague balanced-market statement.
Do not ignore a dominant lead driver just because the average stack is moderate.
Do not present the synthesis as trader advice.
outputRequirements: Quote the average pressure and the lead-driver score.
Explain why the posture is not more defensive or more aggressive.

notes: Dashboard synthesis stays buyer-side and translates cross-channel math into execution cadence.
Energy remains the dominant lane until crude volatility cools further or another channel overtakes it on score and breadth.
